food_subgroup: Fresh spring roll with braised turnip filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Singapore Popiah. Nearest parent is a fresh spring roll or wheat flour wrap. A new FoodOn concept is recommended for this culturally distinct Singaporean dish.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pastry and wraps — fresh spring roll with vegetable filling (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A05BA (pastry and wraps) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for Singapore-style fresh popiah with braised jicama filling.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Singapore Popiah or fresh spring roll as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Singapore Popiah is 2 rolls (approximately 280 g), as commonly served at hawker centres and Peranakan restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Popiah (fresh) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05020083
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database under Food Code F05020083 for Popiah (fresh). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from this verified canonical record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of wheat flour-based wraps and fresh spring rolls — University of Sydney GI Database; GI adjusted downward based on high vegetable fibre content from jicama and bean sprouts
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Popiah in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 is estimated from the closest analogous food: thin wheat flour wrap (GI ~50–55), adjusted downward to approximately 48 based on the high dietary fibre content from jicama (bangkuang) and bean sprouts, which are known to moderate postprandial glucose response. Confidence 0.9. Glycaemic load per 2-roll serving (280 g) calculated as (48 × 16.0 × 2.80) / 100 ≈ 21.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 308 mg per 100 g; approximately 862 mg per standard 2-roll serving (280 g), representing ~37% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one roll per serving and avoid adding extra sweet dark sauce or chilli paste, which contribute additional sodium; popiah remains one of the lower-sodium hawker options compared to noodle soups and fried rice dishes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~48) and moderate glycaemic load (~21.5 per 2-roll serving); high dietary fibre from jicama and bean sprouts moderates postprandial glucose absorption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fresh popiah is a suitable hawker option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI, high vegetable fibre content, and moderate carbohydrate load; portion control to one roll and avoidance of excess sweet sauce is recommended to keep glycaemic load within target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.9 g per 100 g), zero trans fat, and high vegetable content with antioxidant micronutrients; cholesterol is moderate at 32 mg per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh popiah is a heart-friendly hawker choice given its low saturated fat, zero trans fat, and high vegetable fibre content; the inclusion of jicama, bean sprouts, and tofu supports a cardioprotective dietary pattern, though sodium intake should be monitored in those with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vegetable content from jicama, bean sprouts, and lettuce provides anti-inflammatory antioxidants; low saturated fat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fresh popiah is one of the most anti-inflammatory hawker choices due to its high vegetable content and low saturated fat; regular inclusion in a balanced diet may support reduction of systemic inflammation in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore Popiah is a fresh (not fried) spring roll of Teochew-Hokkien and Peranakan origin, widely regarded as one of Singapore's most distinctive and nutritionally balanced hawker snacks. It consists of a thin, soft wheat flour skin (popiah skin) wrapped around a generous filling of slowly braised jicama (bangkuang or yam bean) and carrot, crisp bean sprouts, fried tofu strips, egg omelette strips, small prawns, and crushed roasted peanuts, smeared with hoisin sauce, sweet dark sauce, and chilli paste. Some versions include Chinese dried sausage (lap cheong). Unlike the deep-fried spring roll, fresh popiah is lighter, more vegetable-forward, and lower in fat. Per 100 g, it provides 119 kcal, 5.2 g protein, 4.0 g fat (0.9 g saturated), and 16.0 g carbohydrate with 2.2 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 308 mg per 100 g (~862 mg per 2-roll serving). The GI is estimated at approximately 48 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. It is dairy-free but contains gluten, eggs, shellfish, peanuts, and soy. Popiah is commonly eaten at hawker centres, Peranakan restaurants, and during Chinese New Year celebrations. It is a culturally significant dish in Singapore's multicultural food heritage.
common_user_queries: fresh popiah vs fried spring roll which is healthier
how many calories per popiah roll
is popiah good for weight loss

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F05020083), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. All per-100g values are verified and canonical. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database with documented adjustment basis; confidence 0.9 for GI estimate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Singapore Popiah is not reported in the HPB Foodo Database (Food Code F05020083) as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Foodo Database for this food code. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as confirmed by HPB source (confirmed zero). GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on thin wheat flour wrap from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted downward for high vegetable fibre content; this is not a direct measurement. Glycaemic load per 2-roll serving (280 g) = (48 × 16.0 × 2.80) / 100 = 21.5 g. Halal status: traditional popiah may contain pork-derived ingredients (lap cheong, lard in skin); halal-certified versions are available but not universal — consumers should verify with individual vendors. The food is not suitable for those with peanut, shellfish, gluten, egg, or soy allergies.
